quotes.text: The morning began, as all decent mornings do, with a deep and abiding suspicion that the toaster was working against me. I have never trusted an appliance that gets hot and makes decisions on my behalf. You put the bread in, you get back a VERDICT — and I have never once been found innocent. This is not breakfast. It is a hostile interrogation with a crumb tray.
quotes.speaker: Hunter S. Thompson
quotes.context: A characteristically unhinged 1998 column on domestic life
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the paranoid grandiosity aimed at a harmless household object is the Hunter S. Thompson register exactly. The tell: the real Hunter would've escalated into the federal government by sentence two; this one stays in the kitchen.

quotes.text: Over the past forty years I've been loaded on booze, coke, acid, Quaaludes, glue, cough mixture, heroin, Rohypnol, Klonopin, Vicodin, and too many other heavy-duty substances to list in this footnote. On more than a few occasions I was on all of these at the same time.
quotes.speaker: Ozzy Osbourne
quotes.context: From a footnote in his 2010 autobiography 'I Am Ozzy' (with Chris Ayres), cataloguing four decades of his own substance abuse.

quotes.text: People ask me the secret to a long life. I tell 'em: ignore every single thing your doctor says, marry somebody who can cook, and never, EVER trust a man who doesn't sweat. A dry man is hiding something. That's not wisdom — that's sixty years of paying attention and zero years of listening.
quotes.speaker: Redd Foxx
quotes.context: On longevity, a raunchy 1970s nightclub set
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the blunt, cantankerous life-advice with a filthy-adjacent kicker is Redd Foxx to the bone. The tell: the real Redd would've been far more explicit; this one keeps its shirt on.

quotes.text: Frank used to call me at four in the morning to rehearse. I'd pick up, listen for a second, and say 'Frank, it's a saloon song, not the moon landing — sing it sleepy, that's the whole trick.' Then I'd hang up and go back to doing absolutely nothing, which, for the record, I have perfected into an art form.
quotes.speaker: Dean Martin
quotes.context: On his friendship with Sinatra, a 1970s variety-show chat
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the laid-back 'I've perfected doing nothing' shtick and the Sinatra needle are the Dean Martin persona. The tell: the real Dean would've worked a punchline in half the words.

quotes.text: People keep asking how I made it to a hundred. A cigar, a nap, and absolutely no opinion of my own about anything that matters. Every doctor I ever had told me to quit the cigars. I quit the doctors instead. Simplest decision I ever made — and look, here I am, still ruining everybody's actuarial tables.
quotes.speaker: George Burns
quotes.fake_note: I made this one up — the cigar-and-spite longevity bit and the dry 'I quit the doctors' inversion are George Burns's deadpan. The tell: the real George would've landed it with a perfectly-timed puff and a one-liner half this long.

quotes.text: There are 400,000 words in the English language, and there are seven of them you can't say on television. What a ratio that is. 399,993 to 7. They must really be bad. Shit, piss, fuck, cunt, cocksucker, motherfucker, and tits.
quotes.speaker: George Carlin
quotes.context: Carlin's landmark "Seven Words You Can Never Say on Television" routine from his 1972 album Class Clown, listing the broadcast-banned words verbatim and reveling in saying them all out loud.

source.title: 'Class Clown' — Seven Words You Can Never Say on Television
